commit f9a69e0c367bebcbfdf2a6f295c7389a3295f5b2
Author: JavierusTk <JavierusL2@cyber.es>
Date:   2025-12-18 22:35:36 +0100

    docs: add mORMot2 Software Architecture Design documentation
    
    Add comprehensive documentation for mORMot2:
    
    - Software Architecture Design (SAD) guide with 26 chapters covering:
      - Framework overview and unit structure
      - Core units, cryptography, networking, database access
      - ORM, REST, client-server architecture
      - SOA, interfaces, services
      - MVC, web applications, hosting
      - Security, scripting, ECC, DDD, testing
      - Installation and cross-platform support
    
    - AI-oriented documentation (CLAUDE.md) for each src/ subfolder:
      - app, core, crypt, db, ddd, lib, misc, net
      - orm, rest, script, soa, tools, ui
    
    - FOLDER-DEPENDENCIES.md describing package relationships
    
    These docs are designed to help developers (human and AI) understand
    the mORMot2 architecture quickly and effectively.

Date:   2025-11-19 14:52:47 +0100

    crypt: introducing MAX_PBKDF2_ROUNDS constant
    - the maximum number of PBKDF2 rounds which may trigger a DoS attack
    - 5,0000,000 rounds seems a good upper value for SHA-1
    - 1–5 seconds with SHA-NI is noticeable to be painful
    - used for "modular crypt" hashes

commit 1643cd016f2ebfb46c985784bbeda9c095d9de16
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2025-11-18 21:33:52 +0100

    db: TMongoDBClient.OpenAuth() backward compatibility tip
    - mORMot <= 2.3 TMongoDBDatabase.CreateUser() did wrongly use MongoPasswordDigest() as password and forced SCRAM-SHA-1
    - this is not the expected default behavior
    - use saSha1 and MongoPasswordDigest(UsedName, Password) to authenticate with such wrongly initialized DB user
    - it may be a critical point about https://github.com/synopse/mORMot2/issues/415 changes

commit ffbd8c9ad68bd759ea3f6ae4d0a39d828f38c2c6
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2025-09-06 18:45:42 +0200

    rest: "Modular Crypt" support in TRestClientAuthenticationDefault
    - and all associated TRestClientUri methods
    - the user should be able to ask the server for the "Modular Crypt" parameters of a given user, to safely compute the challenge on its side, potentially for a long time, then send the result to the server
    - the hash won't be computed by the server, so this pattern is safe and don't suffer from potential DoS attacks on server side (the server only makes a fast SHA-256 to validate the challenge)
    - thanks to the "Modular Crypt" patterns, you can tune the password strength for each user, e.g. fast from mobile clients, or very long (some seconds of computation and 1GB of RAM via SCrypt) for admin/root users

commit 7d8fe149eb1a239483ca60727f4a7620aaa6f23b
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2025-09-05 16:53:11 +0200

    crypt: allow bigger N for SCrypt
    - up to LogN=21 consumming 2GB of RAM and 3 seconds of computation
    - SCrypt author and current practice advice for LogN=20 to consume 1GB of RAM and take 1.23 seconds
    - not a good option for password storage on server side, but fine for local key derivation (e.g. file encryption), or if you use a SCRAM-like feature and the server is able to send the stored salt and rounds for actual computation only on the client side - to be continued :)

commit 9c8a6768cc86447bd4afd4a49798d73e082c09a8
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2025-09-02 10:40:03 +0200

    crypt: fixed CryptDataForCurrentUser() per-user local file name on Windows
    - Windows file systems are case-insensitive, so using base64-uri encoding for the file name is unsafe, and may collide between users
    - so we switched to a safer hexadecimal file name pattern
    - existing key files will be renamed with the new name at runtime, so existing password should continue to work seamlessly

commit 46438936ceba99c37e4972cc397f719919da8e86
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2025-08-29 21:24:44 +0200

    core: fixed GetMemAligned() size alignment
    - blocks >= 128KB will be aligned to 64KB
    - blocks >= 4MB will be aligned to 2MB (= PMD_SIZE)
    - the previous formula on POSIX was wrong: too much memory was asked to mmap()

commit 8e7dabd7901c6e6d5b51984a7d5ad185bf5dde00
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2025-08-29 18:54:05 +0200

    core: experimental explicit Huge Pages support on Linux
    - the Huge Pages pool is void by default on most distros, and affects only mremap
    - so it is disabled but you may experiment using OSLINUXHUGEPAGES conditional
    - in practice, THP is the preferred way and aligning to PMD_SIZE is enough
    - our testing did show no performance improvement, and the need to tune the system

Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2025-08-29 18:51:21 +0200

    core: align huge GetMemAligned() requests to 2MB on POSIX
    - to trigger Transparent Huge Pages (THP) on Linux, and Superpages on FreeBSD
    - Windows has no such automatic feature

commit c4eb37a3707d1e58e16eada40a6c11b7a13b6f60
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>

commit e088a919541465587fad5c799eed8f9f4bd6ff78
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2025-08-29 18:47:03 +0200

    core: disable MAP_POPULATE on Medium and Large mmap() calls
    - it is way slower to execute, which is a concern within the Medium blocks giant lock
    - small and medium blocks are usually sparsed accessed
    - even Large blocks won't necessarly benefit from this flag, but if your memory block is ensured to be fully accessed very soon
    - we rather use a new conditional to enable it for large blocks

commit 6eb84fca9153fd971a8ade79c19a2f137321aac9
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2025-08-21 12:40:36 +0200

    crypt: new Random128() function
    - Lecuyer's is finally not a good option to generate IV or MAC, due to the predictability of its LFSR generator
    - we define our own AES-CTR thread safe instance, seeded but distinct from our main TAesPrng, with a 64-bit counter
    - idea is to have the same cryptographic level than our main TAesPrng, but without compromising the state of this main CSPRNG which is used for very sensitive materials (like private key), since IV or MAC are likely to be published

commit 4152454e7f6295fba9b736080ea4e0e7e4dbdad8

    crypt: TAesCtrOsl is not faster on i386 any more

commit e5422232fb0f7d4292aa498675c74511a7994a2a
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2025-08-17 08:00:35 +0200

    crypt: experimental AES-CTR alternative code on x86_64
    - our naive/simple x4 approach - which did wonders on i386 - is a bit slower on 86_64 for no obvious reason - perhaps newer CPUs may benefit from it
    - we also tried an x8 unrolled version to leverage the additional xmm8-xmm15 registers available on the platform - but it was actually slower than our x4 code
    - "simple" x4 and x8 code has been less tested than original AesNiEncryptCtrNist32
    - we continue to use our original AesNiEncryptCtrNist32, and keep our naive/simple x4 and x8 code as disabled reference, and may retry with future CPUs

commit fe0d554196f900a218aac4f9298bf884935b2ea1
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2025-07-21 18:43:35 +0200

    core: fixed / full rewrite of AesNiHashXmm0 to be truly 128-bit
    - the GoLang reference code was truncated the seed and the result to 64-bit
    - the 128-bit seed is fully used now (GoLang used a 64-bit seed)
    - previous implementation did wrongly return highest 64-bit of hash filled with 0 for buffers > 128 bytes, which was just fine on GoLang purpose returning 64-bit results, but wrong about our expectations
    - x86_64 asm now follows the same exact algorithm than i386
    - no performance penalty noticeable, even if x86_64 loop is smaller (still faster than other algorithms including crc32c)
    - less overhead on Win64 for buffers up to 64 bytes (when XMM6-XMM15 are not used)
    - shorter, cleaner and more auditable code, also with more useful comments about the algorithm

commit ec9cf6c93d22075069999411ae55a52f0c69f16f
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2025-07-21 18:41:15 +0200

    core: fixed long standing issue on i386 AesNiHash() core process
    - smallest buffers of size 1..16 may be not properly hashed, with potential collisions

commit fae02efd1a0283b1db9f54c3f2151a5173bfdb07
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2025-07-21 11:45:04 +0200

    core: always check for actual RDTSC availability
    - code should ensure that cfTSC is included in CpuFeatures flags since this opcode may trigger a GPF if CR4.TSD bit is set on hardened systems
    - see https://tizee.github.io/x86_ref_book_web/instruction/rdtsc.html
    and https://blog.cr0.org/2009/05/time-stamp-counter-disabling-oddities.html

    crypt: refactor TBigInt.IsPrime
    - we can use the global SharedRandom Lecuyer generator instance, and avoid per-thread initialization of an instance
    - in the Miller-Rabin algorithm, most time is indeed not spent within the transient random generation, but in the much slower ModPower() or Reduce() calculation functions
    - also quickly detect the very common 65537 value, which is the standard FIPS exponent for RSA

    - only HMAC-SHA-256 and PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA-256 are still part of mormot.crypt.core.pas, because these algorithms are directly used within this unit as default safe key derivation for our AES classes

commit 64816ec50efa7a1915cc229ec9c7c59a5d9f30c2
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2025-03-12 18:18:23 +0100

    crypt: prefer our own ECC-256 generator for private secrets
    - ephemeral keys (e.g. in ECDHE) could still use (much) faster OpenSSL instead

commit 5eda6ff30e873ec24edba4fef5164bfccace40ab
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2025-03-12 18:15:51 +0100

    crypt: made ecc_make_key_pas() secret generation stronger
    - this function is not optimized for performance, but for secrecy: the private key is generated with a very safe SHA-256 diffusion of 1024-bit of randomness from the Operating System and our TAesPrng
    - our idea was to minimize the chances that two consecutive key generations have any similarity, even if performance is not the ultimate goal

commit 8dcd1e160a8c0f252be5c672160257649ac11e85
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2025-03-12 18:11:41 +0100

    core: FillSystemRandom() fix on POSIX
    - only up to 32 bytes (256-bit) are retrieved from the blocking /dev/random
    - as stated by "man urandom" and common sense
    - /dev/urandom and getrandom syscall are not changed, and still can retrieve up to 256 bytes (2048-bit) - as documented by "man getrandom"
    - another more bytes is in fact pointless, and the output Buffer will be padded from our "good enough" Lecuyer's gsl_rng_taus2 generator

commit 74d916fa79d617f8b62979c169079c991c2117ad
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>

    core: refined XorOsEntropy() on both Windows and POSIX
    - to achieve better entropy gathering, with as few syscalls as possible

commit 9c45bef76bfc397a50c8f99995e84b408ccdec1f
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2025-03-12 17:55:15 +0100

    core: enhanced XorEntropy() function
    - will call the OS API only once at startup
    - to populate 256-bit of initial entropy, with forward diffusion of the secret between calls
    - seems lighter, and fair enough for the purpose of this function, which is not expected to be as strong as XorOsEntropy()
    - on Intel/AMD, will use RDTSC and RDRAND opcodes and followed by AesNiHash128, so will be strong enough for our Lecuyer generator

commit 3cfae519b1a2eca803ac2fede852ede8215616bc
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2025-03-06 15:29:48 +0100

    net: changed THttpPartials threading model
    - use a R/W lock for proper parallel work on the files
    - the progressive file reponses will eventually open and close their file within a Read lock
    - the main methods (like Add, Abort or Remove) will use the Write lock
    - to fix e.g. "unable to rename" error during WGet() with background processing

commit 39f8263e439963d86a4493bdb3292cab675bf556
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2024-10-04 22:51:23 +0200

    huge change: TClonable and TSynPersistent
    - TL;WR: use TSynPersistent for DTOs and TClonable for custom Assign()
    - TSynPersistent is the new TObjectWithProps, i.e. a TObject with RTTI and a virtual constructor
    - TClonable has Assign/AssignTo methods to copy its content
    - so we split the TPersistent dual features into two classes, to better follow the SOLID principles
    - TObjectWithRttiMethods is the class dedicated to full RTTI/JSON customization, e.g. as needed by TOrm or TObjectWithID
    - it should still be easy to find your way - 95% of your needs is TSynPersistent

commit fd0aad06b93806793d037304cd2dd7c8e4662a82
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2024-08-28 18:58:35 +0200

    fixed THttpClientSocket on timeout
    - timeout may happen not because the server took its time, but because the network is down (typically, the network cable was removed)
    - sadly, in this case the socket is still reported as OK by the OS (on both Windows and POSIX)
    - no need to retry immediately (we waited up to the timeout, which is enough)
    - but always close the socket, since this particular HTTP request is clearly aborted on this connection

commit eaeaa61bf71927c5857bf088bb1c512390be7173
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2024-06-11 22:29:43 +0200

    maximum/optimum socket output buffer is 128KB on 32-bit

commit 1443bc7a05d4d01cb1a33da74d422737c1b0f3f2
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2024-06-11 22:25:03 +0200

    reduce HTTP server memory consumption when serving files
    - on Linux/POSIX, typical values for the output buffers are 2MB for TCP, 200KB on Unix Sockets
    - in practice, 256KB is good enough, and anything bigger won't make any performance benefit, but would consume much more memory
    - only noticeable with a lot of concurrent client connections serving huge files

Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>

    - instead of fixed 0 which may allow attacks on our hash tables with some forged values
    - only needed with xxhash32 or crc32c hashes (i.e. arm/aarch or very old Intel), since our AesNiHash on Intel/AMD is already resilient to such attacks

commit c53dbd99076596ef84689e209a150138490d4f13
Merge: 073eba6ed ee6f7fcc7
Author: ab <webcontact01@synopse.info>
Date:   2024-03-01 12:15:00 +0100

    Merge pull request #242 from sakura1977/RFC-9239
    
    Follow RFC 9239 mime type definitions.

commit ee6f7fcc79d34cd031d4ff8989d91b5d1a2e65c0
Author: sakura1977 <59915973+sakura1977@users.noreply.github.com>
Date:   2024-03-01 11:21:15 +0100

    Javascript Modules
    
    RFC 9239 defines .mjs files to be handled like .js files. JavaScript modules have become a standard in web applications and should be supported.
    
    https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc9239
    https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9239

commit 724a71c480d1f332041951da9014288e0c2c81da
Author: sakura1977 <59915973+sakura1977@users.noreply.github.com>
Date:   2024-03-01 11:19:21 +0100

    text/javascript
    
    RFC 9239 defined "text/javascript" as mime content type for JS-files, "application/javascript" is defined as obsolete.
    
    https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc9239
    https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9239

commit 073eba6ed53ee76c84adb2ffee8da39723c098a6

commit ea798e95716a9e45da9c8b7c7888cb66192791e2
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2024-03-01 10:39:26 +0100

    fixed race condition of async ProcessWrite() in a background thread
    - when the client is making a lot of requests on the loopback (i.e. only regression tests, not production)
    - should not appear with normal network latency and regular workload
    - observed via LUTI and by increasing RepeatTextArray() tests to successive calls
    - now RepeatTextArray() tests with 50000 successive calls pass with no problem

commit 0d04312b2f36cbaf9edc6fa8d453965ff1a0b508
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2024-02-19 10:47:25 +0100

    get rid of TWinIocp.GetWaiting
    - it sounds like a problem in the Windows socket layer itself
    - when `wrk` is used with e.g. 10,000 concurrent connections on Windows 7, the sockets are closed on the client side, but not on the server side: they remain unnotified in the IOCP queue, and if we check them manually using a recv() we got a "retry" meaning that the socket is still there, ready to receive data, whereas it has been closed on the server side
    - it may be a problem of the Windows 7 socket queue, or of the VM bridge interface
    - I will continnue my investigations

commit bdfa0125f8103b388779457ee9ef8a37eb3cf566

    - but this does not fix anything: ProcessRead get recv()=Retry on the sockets

commit 143e82165294e5ff3aad5b95c310e8b24ab2c01a
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2024-01-30 11:18:48 +0100

    circumvent FPC RawByteString concatenation bug
    - FPC RTL does not handle RawByteString as "system/normal string" but refuses to concatenate them with existing AnsiString
    - this is not expected, nor compatible with Delphi
    - so we need to force CP_UTF8 everywhere in the framework when a returned RawByteString may be used as a plain string, e.g. with concatentation, by the user code (e.g. StringFromFile, network receive buffers, or decompression/decryption functions)
    - it won't hurt RawByteString process anyway, if you expect binary content, because UTF-8 is encoded as binary/AnsiString
    - another option may be to use Make([]) for concatenation, but it is less obvious

Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2023-12-21 15:17:01 +0100

    fixed PeerCache UDP discovery on POSIX
    - we need to bind to the broadcast address to receive brodcast messages
    - but then local IP packets are not received: so we need to send the responses to the broadcast IP if the destination OS is marked as POSIX
    - Windows is non-conformant, but easier to work with: if you bind to a local IP, you receive the broadcast messages

commit c4ee17a8b97b86096956e3d2bf910f3f12ccde8a
Merge: e2794597d d2dab8bc1
Author: ab <webcontact01@synopse.info>
Date:   2023-11-27 08:43:38 +0100

    Merge pull request #227 from nortg/master
    
    Fixed critical rtti cache bug

commit d2dab8bc1359f7d4800d8e7a0f1bd7735e0deffa
Author: nortg <48905359+nortg@users.noreply.github.com>
Date:   2023-11-26 10:09:08 +0100

    Fixed critical rtti cache bug
    
    TRttiInfo.ComputeCache method assumes Cache parameter will be zeroed out by caller but this is not true because of usage of out prefix which discards initial value.
    The method does not correctly initialize Cache structure therefor it contains whatever garbage is currently on the stack. The only time out parameter would "work" is if Cache would contain another managed type field which would force the compiler to initialize the structure on method entry.
    
    Fix is simple, use var prefix so that Cache is passed as reference. To be honest because of importance of this method in RTTI generation I would not leave it up to the caller to correctly initialize the Cache record.

commit 703ad9711891f46ee937f7ea8c6a7f77a75c9b70
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2023-10-20 18:17:04 +0200

    include all needed tests for our X.509 PKI
    - the whole Certification Path Validation is checked
    - PEM persistence is also validadated
    - and benchmark is amazing: loading a DER + validating its FULL CHAIN up to the CA is around 4,800,000 certificates per second, for raw DER binary to full validation
    - the FULL CHAIN is validated each time: we don't cheat by putting a naive cache which is likely to give awful false positives - we only cache what can't evolve, like a cryptographic digital signature, or a revocation order

commit 81202adb5abcc2928881ff8bb0a453c056e6bc96
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2023-10-06 15:04:41 +0200

    hardened RSA/BigInt prime generation
    - randomness is the main weak point for RSA prime generation
    - has been proven with IoT generators: https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/9014350
    - start from several trusted sources of random, including the OS and the CPU RdRand opcode, then finalize using our TAesPrng
    - ensure 1/3 of the bits are set (even if it never occured after 1000000000 trials with our TAesPrng)
    - ensure FIPS requirements are always met, even in the very unlikely case of InAdd(2) overflow

commit 060d5be06d91f54bb53138fda86fc6c58f9adebe
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2023-08-31 12:08:13 +0200

    fixed random blocking of async server
    - asynchronous and multi-thread programming is hard - race conditions could be difficult to track
    - I spent a lot of time on this issue, which was reproduced using the wrk tool and a lot of concurrent connections for a long time
    - it was more easily reproduced on Windows, but could potentially affect all platforms, and may be the culprit of very rare race condition on other platforms like Linux (seen e.g. on TFB)
    - in fact, it was a one-liner - as often with race conditions :) - just move one flag assignment at the right place

commit 3b1b6dfa86e382d1ccf8c182d9858592187005a6
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2023-08-24 18:09:41 +0200

    force acoThreadSmooting on Windows
    - because without it the async server seems unstable and consumes too much CPU in its R0 thread - see https://synopse.info/forum/viewtopic.php?id=6682
    - this is a temporary solution until something better is reported (and needed)
    - idea is to use the plain THttpSocketServer on Windows, and use the async code on Linux only (where it seems very stable and efficient)
    - writing cross-platform multi-thread IO is hard for sure :)

commit eb4123ac07e8975cfdb425f5f06662ce389fefcf
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2023-08-09 18:07:17 +0200

    rewritten the internal RTTI hash tables
    - regroup TRttiKind tables into less slots
    - store two hash tables, one for PRttiInfo, another for Name lookup
    - use a O(1) hash algorithm for PRttiInfo (often called), and O(n) for Name (seldom called)
    - added addition thread-safe non-blocking per-slot caches
    - fixed thread safety for the most paranoid process

Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2023-08-08 18:40:15 +0200

    introducing NOVMTPATCH conditional
    - to disable the vmtAutoTable slot runtime patch, replacing it with a slowest algo
    - could be used e.g. when in-memory executables can't be patched (e.g. on security constrained systems, or on OS without any mmap support)
    - fallback to the regular Rtti.FindType() lookup which is not O(1) and could still be optimized

    - from a shared connection

    - could eventually be cached

    - proper ASN.1 constants and debug dump
    - enhanced TLdapAttribute format recognition: FileTime values, iso values
    - new TSearchResultList.SearchTimeMicroSec property (also in debugging dump)

Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2023-03-20 10:46:52 +0100

    BREAKING CHANGE: TRawUtf8List is not thread-safe by default
    - unless fThreadSafe is set to use the TRWLock
    - as in mORMot 1
    - so sligthly faster, and comparable to TStringList by default
    - also rewritten not PUREMORMOT2 classes TRawUtf8ListLocked/TRawUtf8ListHashed/TRawUtf8ListHashedLock to behave exactly as in mORMot 1
    - our previous implementation was clearly confusing, especially when coming from mORMot 1, and even non-compatible when porting existing mORMot 1 applications

commit 725087dc42778237921278dccf2e99c13e7cb2d2
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2023-03-02 10:42:00 +0100

    changed /rawupdates SQL query
    - ORM-like nested SELECT is replaced by " update table set randomNumber = CASE id when $1 then $2 when $3 then $4 ... when $9 then $10 else randomNumber end where id in ($1,$3,$5,$7,$9) "
    - this weird syntax gives best number for TFB /rawupdates?queries=20 but is not good for smaller or higher count
    - we won't include it in the ORM but only for our RAW results - as other frameworks (e.g. ntex) do

    on Linux, TSynEvent will use eventfd() instead of PRTLEvent
    - eventfd() in blocking and non-semaphore mode reduces the syscalls to simple read/write/poll
    - seems to be stable in practice - feedback is welcome!

commit 8d333f5300c8f958f46ff67010e83c811a5281e1
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2023-01-30 17:26:09 +0100

    ensure TPollSocketEpoll is thread-safe
    - we assume thread-safety in mormot.net.async but in fact it was not due to an internal dynamic array for results
    - using a local stack-allocated array of 256 items is perfectly thread-safe and also faster (on my PC)
    - is likely to fix error reported with high number of CPUs at https://synopse.info/forum/viewtopic.php?pid=38921#p38921

commit d9cace9e347f23443dbcab47c36e8c8b5c8e8195
Author: pavel.mash <pavel.mash@inbase.com.ua>
Date:   2023-01-21 21:04:10 +0200

    Solved TFB /rawqueries and /rawupdates performance problems:
    - Postgres pipeline mode has been rethought - better to use Flush after each statement instead of PipelineSync after lase statement; In this case server starts to execute queries ASAP;
    - TFB /rawupdates - Use `::bigint[]` instead fo `::NUMERIC[]` typecast (NUMERIC is floating point type, nut we need Int64); add `order by id` to minimize locks waits (as in ORM)

commit 4967242579538e11d133e0cf6c8e41624be35a65
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2023-01-19 20:00:40 +0100

    initialize a main Windows Job for agl to kill all sub-process when main is killed
    - all sub-processes will now be part of this Windows Job unless soWinJobCloseChildren is set, so RunRedirect() will use the CREATE_BREAKAWAY_FROM_JOB flag, then creates its own new Windows Job
    - makes agl much stronger than NSSM in case of hard killing of the main service process: everything is linked and killed by Windows itself

commit 5409041732140128224178b00b727f1c1f4b2b77
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2022-12-31 15:31:39 +0100

    new adaptative TAsyncConnections.ThreadPollingWakeup algorithm
    - the naive/standard/well-used algorithm of waking up the threads on need
    does not perform well, especially with a high number of CPU cores: the
    global CPU usage remains idle, because most of the time is spent between
    the threads, and not procesing actual data
    - we actually wake up the sub-threads only if it did not become idle within
    GetTickCount64 resolution (i.e. 16ms on Windows, 4ms on Linux)
    - on small load or quick response, only R1 thread is involved
    - on slow process (e.g. remote DB access), R1 is identified as blocking, and
    R2..Rmax threads are awaken only if WAKEUP_LOAD events have been assigned
    to processing threads
    - it seems to leverage the CPU performance especially when the number of
    threads is higher than the number of cores, or on high number of cores CPU
    - this algorithm seems efficient, and simple enough to implement and debug,
    in respect to what I have seen in high-performance thread pools (e.g. in
    MariaDB), which have much bigger complexity (like a dynamic thread pool)

commit 8c9d77cfb7ebcec10b3581d54e874a8e996ace00
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2022-12-22 22:06:52 +0100

    Custom URI Routing using an efficient Radix Tree
    - the TUriTree class allows both URI rewrite and callback execution
    - can parse parameters within the URI, e.g. /user/<id>/picture, either published to the callback, or used to rewrite the URI
    - use a Radix Tree data structure for very efficient lookup, even with hunderths of registered URI
    - no memory allocation is made during the process, unless mandatory (e.g. to rewrite the URI)
    - process is fully thread-safe and parallized
    - this router design is unique in the context of Delphi and FPC frameworks, and outperforms them by a huge margin
    - may eventually be used for the TRestServer itself, for all URIs, including remote ORM, interface-based services and method-based services

commit 6ff91915a7136a2da21cfef3de26873960629040
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2022-12-16 12:55:50 +0100

    introducing very efficient TOSLightLock
    - calls the Slim Reader/Writer (SRW) Win32 API in exclusive mode or directly the pthreads library in non-recursive/fast mode on Linux
    - on XP, where SRW are not available, fallback to a TLightLock
    - on non-Linux POSIX, fallback to regular cthreads/TRTLCriticalSection
    - same signature as TOSLock/TLightLock, usable as compile time alternatives
    - numbers make it a better alternative to TLightLock, when some contention occurs (e.g. our async server gives 15% better RPS on Linux)

commit e3aa967349845e03c6bd8ce554320f04fab3aa6c
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2022-12-12 11:13:33 +0100

    new Benchmark entry for collections - including RTL
    - our test case consists in adding, getting, enumerating, then searching
    - to be fair, for basic features (adding + getting + enumerating), mORMot generics are slightly slower than the latest Delphi generics.collection unit - but faster than FPC generics anyway
    - mORMot generics increases the .dcu size much less than Delphi's RTL
    e.g. size of test.core.collections.dcu with Delphi 11.1 on Win32:
        default mormot.core.generics:       24 KB
        NOSPECIALIZE mormot.core.generics:  47 KB
        default mormot.core.generics + Delphi generics.collection: 199 KB
        (for only 4 generics collections definition)
     - but our extended test with value searching let mORMot outperforms both Delphi and FPC RTL, even more the FPC generics which use the very slow AnsiCompareStr() call - for no benefit, and no Delphi compatibility

commit 7bbf42163b2262cdea8148508997bb95b09e0de3
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2022-10-14 18:35:08 +0200

    new TRestStorageInMemory.TrackChanges feature
    - allow to synchronize on need an in-memory storage into an IRestOrm persistence layer, e.g. once in a while
    - is simpler and faster than ORM-level master/slave replication, and perfect for a high-performance server maintaining its state in a TRestStorageInMemory, but pushing it into an external database in the background (e.g. once per second)
    - actual IRestOrm persistence will use a TRestBatch so will be very efficient

commit 044766c615b6cde391bc59b79354453084a6cb82
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2022-09-23 17:10:03 +0200

    HTTP-01 Let's Encrypt Challenges HTTP Server on port 80
    - TAcmeLetsEncryptServer will handle one or several Let's Encrypt domains certificates with an associated HTTP server running on port 80
    - will support HTTP-01 challenge answers when renewing is active
    - will redirect any plain HTTP port 80 request to HTTPS port 443 URI
    - at startup, then twice a day, will try to renew the certificates in the background, following RenewBeforeEndDays property policy

    let THttpServer handle ServerThreadPoolCount < 0 value
    - would use a single thread to rule them all: accept + requests as HTTP/1.0 connections, and never span any sub-thread
    - may be defined when the server is expected to have very low usage
    - e.g. for port 80 to 443 redirection or to implement Let's Encrypt HTTP-01 challenges (also on port 80) using the newly introduced OnHeaderParsed callback

commit c8486a361ea645905a9b951a705ae6e6fd7d3628

    new FileSetSticky() function

commit a8ebcb64f067ab55396557aadb7d4a1388c4eb7b
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2022-08-31 19:13:53 +0200

    new GetComputerUuid() function
    - to retrieve a genuine 128-bit UUID identifier for this computer
    - first try GetSmbios(sbiUuid), i.e. the SMBIOS System UUID, maybe from the local /var/tmp/.synopse.smb cache file
    - otherwise, will compute a genuine hash from known hardware information (CPU, Bios, physical MAC addresses) and store it in a local file for the next access, e.g. into '/var/tmp/.synopse.uid' on POSIX
    - so this function will always work, and support the case where you never have user root permissions, but you still need some genuine machine UUID

commit 17c7e439ee9fd9222ce6297c0c97e77b34489646
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2022-08-31 19:12:56 +0200

    fixed Android compilation

commit 28de1575e3a241b6eef5d6cf0acc1c8e6795e5ec
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2022-08-31 19:11:19 +0200

    enhanced GetRawSmbios() to use a local cache on POSIX
    - the current user should have enough rights to read the main system memory, which means it should be root on most POSIX Operating Systems
    - so we persist (compressed and hashed) this raw binary in /var/tmp/.synopse.smb to retrieve it from non-root user
    - this is not needed on Windows, where the API seems to work with no admin rights
    function : boolean;

commit df98e498085c50da1c84d4f561d138bc3c511cf9
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2022-08-20 14:02:29 +0200

    updated libdeflate sources from 1.7 to 1.13
    - to use the gcc-10 compiler (instead of former/old gcc-7 compiler), we had to remove the -ffreestanding flag
    - we observe what is expected with libdeflate 1.13: much faster compression at level 1 (which is perfect for our server use since it is the level used for HTTP/HTTPS gzip content-encoding), better compression ratio at level 6, faster crc32/adler32 hashing, and similar decompression speed
    - see https://github.com/ebiggers/libdeflate/issues/216

Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2022-08-08 19:06:46 +0200

    implemented OP_COMPRESSED message with zlib for MongoDB
    - should safe a lot of bandwidth on realistic production scenario with a separated MongoDB server instance(s)
    - this could make a lot of difference on a cloud VM MongoDB server
    - compression is triggered using new TMongoClient.ZLib* properties
    - when used in conjunction with libdeflate, zlib is a better choice than Snappy

commit 3370b572f04d53d18462619ce495b8aa989eb553
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2022-08-01 10:51:37 +0200

    rewritten THttpRequestContext.ParseHeader
    - standard headers are expected to be pure A-Z chars: fast lowercase search
    - or $20 makes conversion to a-z lowercase, but won't affect - / : chars
    - the worse case may be some false positive, which won't hurt
    - resulting code is much less readable than cascaded IdemPPChar(), but slightly faster ;)

commit cb178e5c9638a467a80830edaf3fd220cd7a5c62
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2022-07-29 19:48:06 +0200

    new TSqlDbConnectionProperties.EnsureColumnNameUnique property
    - by default, result column names won't be checked for name unicity
    - it is up to your SQL statement to return genuine columns
    - ColumName() lookup will use O(1) brute force, so you should rather use per-index Column*() methods
    - force true to have the mORMot 1 compatible behavior, in which column names will be hashed for unicity (so will be slower at execution), but will allow faster ColumnName() lookup

commit 00c3a15a8d4821c4638506b104977c046d4a57ea
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2022-07-25 17:33:04 +0200

    removed internal list of unsubscribed tags for async sockets
    - this list intend was an attempt to stabilize HTTP/1.0 process, but it was in fact not a good idea at all :(
    - it triggered random GPF and memory leaks on highly-concurrent HTTP/1.0 connections, because TAsyncConnection pointers were reused by the MM whereas the pending results tags were still not flushed, so those new connections were in fact ignored... and eventually leaked...
    - to be fair, it was not a very realistic use case, but "wrk -c 16384" was able to reproduce it, if you set the server keep alive parameter to 1 second, or with Apache Bench in HTTP/1.0 mode
    - resulting code seems now pretty stable from HTTP/1.1 or HTTP/1.0 requests
    - as a side benefit, it also enhances performance, because we don't have to make an O(n) over this list any more

Date:   2022-07-22 13:31:44 +0200

    introducing hsoFavorHttp10 HTTP server ProcessOptions
    - by default Async.Clients.ReadWaitMs will be set to 0, for better performance and stability on highly concurrent HTTP/1.1 access (which is the most common case of this server) - this will fix an unexpected heisenbug of memory leak after connection keep alive is flushed
    - use hsoFavorHttp10 explicitly behind a reverse proxy, or use regular THttpServer which was designed for this purpose

commit c38d7f628f9d3c9bbbc084deaf50ec2921556379
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2022-07-21 18:12:03 +0200

    avoid thread contention on TRawUtf8InterningSlot.UniqueFromBuffer
    - i.e. it won't try to acquire the read lock if it is currently in write lock, adding a new string
    - the MM would be used, which won't hurt and is likely to happen in case of a lot of non-unique input (which was not the scope for this interning stuff)
    - so it would avoid DDoS attacks e.g. on forged HTTP headers

commit c579a0896d702e807f8e3ddf6a967d5fd7277cd4
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2022-07-16 10:59:43 +0200

    circumvent Delphi 11 limitation to use our AVX2 x86_64 asm
    - for a huge performance boost on Win64 for IsValidUtf8() and Base-64 encoding/decoding
    - AVX2 ASM .align 32 for const is not available on Delphi :(
    - in practice, loading constants from 16 bytes aligned boundaries is not a huge problem outside of the loop, and AVX2 opcodes allow reading memory from unaligned data - even if it may induce a performance penalty
    - FPC will use the "align 32" best option, and Delphi fallback to "align 16" which is the best it can do for now

commit 320435a5cc3d38016173867154f2eecf599840b6
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2022-07-15 15:04:07 +0200

    new Intel/AMD 64-bit AVX2 asm for SHA-3 hashing
    - on x86_64 and latest FPC/Delphi compilers, will detect and use AVX2 asm if available on the CPU
    - our AVX2 version is at least x2 faster than our optimized KeccakPermutationKernel() x86_64 asm
    - for instance, verifying a JWT with SHA-3 is now faster than SHA-2 or SHA-512 (which require HMAC overhead)
    - inspired by Andy Polyakov's keccak1600-avx2.pl from the CRYPTOGAMS project

commit 9168557b2870eea2325ace01ab97e51cc439e5f9
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2022-07-11 14:36:26 +0200

    reworked Intel ERMBS support on MoveFast/FillCharFast
    - assume ERBMS is available when cnt >= 256 (cpuid flag may not be propagated within VMs)
    - use simple SSE2 loop for 32..256 bytes
    - use ERBMS for 256..512K input (should work on all CPUs, even older with no ERBMS)
    - use non volatile SSE2 or AVX lopp >= 512KB
    - remove backward "std rep movsb" since it is not involved by ERMBS and is always slower
    - applied on both i386 and x86_64 targets - mostly on 64-bit

commit a71011b68000a31ee8dade21ab67c5fc63b2fe33

    - linger boolean flag should be set to "off"

    let HTTP client handle poor HTTP/1.1 (Java) server
    - server sending no Content-Length neither chunk transfer encoding
    - should read until the connection is closed
    - which is very inefficient for sure, but some buggy (Java) server still to this
    - also for HTTP/1.1: https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7230#section-3.3.3 paragraph 7

commit 634acd51dbbaf3ec25237bd5f78cbdcb3a380128
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2022-02-03 17:18:16 +0100

    refactored asm to use "sub" instead of "inc"
    - as Agner states: "The INC and DEC instructions are inefficient on some CPUs because they write to only part of the flags register (excluding the carry flag). Use ADD or SUB instead to avoid false dependences or inefficient splitting of the flags register, especially if they are followed by an instruction that reads the flags."

commit b70aa7e61362c4360257c9cb3fe2448d2b0e96f8
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2022-01-27 10:12:37 +0100

    some buffer allocations refactoring

commit 4708b03356bf64ebbc73b20b19b3715f05baeec9
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2022-01-27 10:05:30 +0100

    huge Win64 asm refactoring to fix random FP issues
    - Windows x64 calling convention expects to preserve XMM6-XMM15 (whereas the Linux/POSIX ABI consider them as volatile)
    - we discovered some hard-to-track incorrect floating point values with high level of optimization (e.g FPC -O3) during our regression tests (typical Heisenbug)
    - so a full asm review and fix was needed
    - resulting performance seems not degradated after this refactoring, thanks to conditional compilation and manual asm tuning
    - we included some small optimizations (e.g. on AES-CTR on a single block)
    - mORMot 1 still suffers from this issue (which is hard/specific to trigger anyway)

commit 13e0df747bb35a379b4e97422b1db26575aa4a8a

Date:   2022-01-26 17:27:07 +0100

    - movntdq was reported to be faster than ERMSB

    - introducing FPCMM_LARGEBIGALIGN align largest chunks to 21-bit=2MB=PMD_SIZE

    - single threaded apps may enhance their performance with the new FPCMM_MEDIUMMAPPOPULATE conditional
    - FPCMM_MEDIUMMAPPOPULATE and FPCMM_LARGEMAPPOPULATE allow to prepare the TLB in mmap()
    - FPCMM_MEDIUM32BIT may reduce memory fragmentation in some usecases

commit 013be075c522380024f7c1e3965dd930ffc6d8ce
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2022-01-11 19:51:25 +0100

    optimzed short-TTL server connections
    - e.g. for HTTP/1.0 requests with small input/output content
    - such connections won't subscribe to socket/poll/epoll unless they need it for complex requests
    - such connections won't register themselves to the internal connections list
    - call optimized accept4() to minimize OS calls on Linux
    - other optimizations and refactoring
    - refreshed samples

Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2021-12-31 18:53:58 +0100

    huge SOA server-side refactoring
    - instances ID are now retrieved with O(log(n)) binary search instead of O(n) brute force - make a huge performance boost when a lot of clients are connected, e.g. in sicClientDriven mode
    - fixed instance _Release with RefCount>1 - by using a separated GC list
    - implemented the new optFreeDelayed option
    - enhanced thread scalability by using non-blocking locks, and smaller dedicated locks
    - enhanced logged content
    - a lot of internal renaming and code consistency fixes to ease future maintainability
    - tuned global SOA performance

Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2021-11-26 16:41:44 +0100

    deep refactoring of TDynArrayHasher
    - it will always maintain its hash table during insert/update not during lookup
    - even a few items would maintain a hash table (consumes only 512 bytes of RAM)
    - will ensure that our hashing algorithms could be used with concurrent lookup, e.g. with TRWLock.ReadOnlyLock multiple reads
    - also refine the internals for safer use and better maintainability
    - main difference is that in "pure mORMOt 2 mode", the ReHash method is renamed ForceRehash

Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>

    TPollSocketsSlot will now use a TRTLCriticalSection
    - our simple atomic counter lock was naive and didn't work as expected on AARCH64
    - we didn't notice any performance impact when switching to direct pthread API calls on Linux
    - using pthread mutex is a more stable and safe solution anyway

commit 10cef0c3edeee786707ac23aaa91c6e23842bce8
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2021-07-29 17:26:07 +0200

    new TJsonQuickParser internal JSON parsing engine
    - based on an efficient state machine with an internal stack to avoid any recursive call
    - used now for IsValidJson() GotoEndJsonItemStrict() JsonArrayCount() JsonObjectPropCount()
    - more compliant, more reusable, and slightly faster (900MB/s)
    - still relaxed on numbers, escaped strings and commas

commit afefdc58e9b0ec3ac001374eb2ca157921ac235c
Author: Arnaud Bouchez <ab@synopse.info>
Date:   2021-07-20 12:25:27 +0200

    properly use shorter movaps/movups rather than movdqa/movdqu opcodes
    - shortest instructions are always fine between memory and register
    - but not between registers for moving the result of an integer instruction
    - as states by Agner Fog "Optimizing Assembly" pdf page 119
